course.title: Production File Delivery with Amazon S3 and CloudFront in Go 1.26
course.description: Go backend developers who can defend production HTTP services and need to build, secure, test, operate, and recover high-volume file and media delivery with Amazon S3 and CloudFront. This original, research-backed path uses repeated retrieval, cumulative artifacts, changed-case labs, stakeholder projects, and a performance exam.
outcomes: Demonstrate go 1.26.5, aws sdk for go v2 1.42.1, s3 1.105.0, transfermanager 0.3.2, cloudfront/sign 1.11.7, http file semantics, s3 object and bucket models, sdk clients, credentials, retries, bounded upload and download, integrity, multipart, direct browser upload, go gateways, metadata transactions, versioning, object lock, lifecycle, iam, encryption, signed access, oac, cloudfront caching, media ranges, edge security, replication, recovery, events, observability, capacity, cost, tests, faults, and release defense through an independently verified stakeholder artifact.

Demonstrate runnable deterministic pure-go storage decision functions plus explicit go compile, vet, race, fuzz, leak, controlled aws integration, load, security, cost, restore, and production transfer gates through an independently verified stakeholder artifact.
Demonstrate five cumulative real-world file-delivery systems and a performance-based production defense examination through an independently verified stakeholder artifact.
sharedRequirements: Every activity retrieves Go, HTTP semantics, accessibility, security, validation, persistence, testing, context, goroutine ownership, resource budgets, and evidence habits before adding one storage or delivery boundary.

Browser Go is deterministic and isolated. It models identity, ranges, policy, integrity, lifecycle, caching, cost, and recovery decisions but never imports AWS SDK packages, opens sockets, contacts AWS or databases, reads host state, executes commands, uses credentials, or causes external effects.

Passing work requires stable file and scenario identity, a named owner and state, bounded bytes, time, attempts, concurrency, cache and cost, observable changed and failure results, cleanup and recovery, and explicit compiler, controlled AWS, security, load, cost, restore, or production transfer limits.

credential.title: Production File Delivery with Amazon S3 and CloudFront in Go 1.26 Certificate

modules.title: File-Delivery Outcomes, Boundaries, and Evidence
modules.description: A public-service portal reports successful uploads and fast downloads but cannot prove that the intended bytes are authorized, durable, retrievable, accessible, affordable, or recoverable. Learners cumulatively produce file-delivery outcome and evidence map through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Define file-delivery success with learner, uploader, viewer, owner, security, accessibility, operations, and finance evidence.
Trace browser, Go service, object store, CDN, identity provider, scanner, database, and stakeholder custody without assigning evidence a boundary cannot observe.
Separate business file identity, object key, object version, representation bytes, metadata, checksum, URL, and cache entry.

modules.title: File Servers, Object Storage, CDNs, and Architecture Selection
modules.description: A product sends every byte through one web process, stores uploads on an ephemeral disk, and adds a CDN without deciding authority, mutation, privacy, or failure boundaries. Learners cumulatively produce architecture and responsibility decision record through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Compare local filesystems, shared filesystems, object storage, databases, origin services, and CDNs by interface, consistency, scale, trust, and operations.
Separate control-plane metadata and authorization from high-volume data transfer while preserving one auditable decision.
Choose server upload, direct upload, redirect, proxy, signed edge delivery, or public delivery from threat, transformation, audit, and performance evidence.

modules.title: HTTP Representations, Validators, Ranges, and File Transfer
modules.description: A download endpoint guesses MIME types, buffers large bodies, ignores validators and Range, and treats Content-Disposition text as trusted HTML. Learners cumulatively produce HTTP file-representation and transfer contract through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Define media type, content length, content encoding, language, disposition, filename parameters, and cache metadata as representation facts.
Apply ETag and Last-Modified validators with If-Match, If-None-Match, If-Range, 304, 412, and changed-state evidence.
Parse and evaluate single byte ranges, suffix ranges, satisfiable bounds, 206, Content-Range, Accept-Ranges, and 416.

modules.title: S3 Objects, Keys, Metadata, Tags, Listing, and Consistency
modules.description: A team encodes tenant authority in free-form key strings, overwrites metadata accidentally, assumes folders are directories, and scans one unpaginated listing as a transaction. Learners cumulatively produce S3 object-model and listing invariant table through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Distinguish bucket, key, version ID, delete marker, upload ID, part number, and application identity.
Design opaque or structured keys with explicit delimiter, normalization, tenant, cardinality, migration, and disclosure rules.
Choose system metadata, user metadata, object tags, database metadata, and event payloads by mutability, limits, query, trust, and lifecycle needs.

modules.title: Bucket Types, Endpoints, Regions, and S3 Express
modules.description: A client hard-codes path-style URLs, assumes global ordering and lifecycle features, and deploys latency-sensitive work without selecting a compatible bucket type or zone. Learners cumulatively produce bucket-type and endpoint compatibility matrix through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Compare general-purpose and directory buckets by namespace, endpoint, zone, ordering, throughput, durability scope, versioning, lifecycle, and feature support.
Construct regional virtual-hosted and zonal endpoints without confusing region, availability zone, bucket name, TLS name, or path style.
Resolve intended region and endpoint explicitly and reject redirects, signing-region drift, and accidental cross-region transfer.

modules.title: Go 1.26 and AWS SDK for Go v2 Client Contract
modules.description: A service copies an SDK v1 tutorial, floats module versions, shares request options incorrectly, and treats generated Go types as runtime validation. Learners cumulatively produce pinned Go and AWS SDK v2 client contract through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Pin Go 1.26.5, aws-sdk-go-v2 1.42.1, S3 1.105.0, CloudFront sign 1.11.7, and tested operating targets as one compatibility contract.
Load immutable shared configuration once, construct scoped clients, pass context, and assign transport and credential lifetimes.
Use SDK pointers, enums, document types, output metadata, paginators, and nil handling without losing API semantics.

modules.title: Credentials, Signing, Retries, Timeouts, and Client Resilience
modules.description: A process accepts static keys from request data, retries every failure beyond its caller deadline, and records neither attempt identity nor ambiguous write state. Learners cumulatively produce bounded S3 request and retry state machine through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Use short-lived credential providers with explicit workload identity, refresh ownership, least privilege, and redaction.
Explain SigV4 canonical request, region, service, signed headers, payload mode, clock, and credential scope without hand-building production signatures.
Compose request, attempt, transfer, and shutdown deadlines and propagate cancellation through body and goroutine ownership.

modules.title: Upload Admission, Untrusted Files, and Quarantine
modules.description: An upload handler trusts client filenames and content types, accepts unlimited bodies, publishes bytes before scanning, and leaves partial state when the caller disconnects. Learners cumulatively produce bounded upload-admission and quarantine workflow through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Enforce per-file, request, tenant, concurrent, time, and aggregate byte budgets before and during transfer.
Treat filenames as display metadata, normalize safely, reject controls and path confusion, and generate storage keys independently.
Combine bounded signature inspection, declared media type, extension, parser or scanner evidence, and allow-list policy without executing content.

modules.title: PutObject, Metadata, Checksums, and Encryption Defaults
modules.description: A service uploads an unbounded reader, uses the returned ETag as a universal digest, loses content metadata on replacement, and cannot prove which bytes S3 accepted. Learners cumulatively produce single-object upload and integrity evidence record through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Own and close input bodies, preserve cancellation, bound bytes, and distinguish request completion from later business publication.
Select supported checksum algorithms, send full-object evidence, validate service response, and retain an application integrity record.
Set and preserve Content-Type, Content-Disposition, Cache-Control, encoding, language, custom metadata, and tags during replacements and copies.

modules.title: AWS Transfer Manager, Concurrency, and Migration
modules.description: A large-transfer service starts new work on deprecated s3/manager, accepts unstable defaults, multiplies concurrency per request, and cannot bound memory, descriptors, or partial failures. Learners cumulatively produce transfer-manager adoption and resource budget through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Adopt feature/s3/transfermanager 0.3.2 as a pinned pre-v1 dependency with explicit compatibility review and rollback.
Inventory deprecated feature/s3/manager use, map behavior and options, characterize outputs, and migrate behind contract tests.
Choose UploadObject, DownloadObject, and GetObject by streaming, seekability, checksum, destination, and ownership requirements.

modules.title: Multipart Upload State, Parts, Checksums, and Cleanup
modules.description: A worker starts multipart uploads without durable state, chooses tiny parts, loses ETags, retries concurrent parts blindly, and never aborts abandoned uploads. Learners cumulatively produce recoverable multipart-upload state machine through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Choose single versus multipart transfer and part size from object size, part-count limits, memory, network, retry, and completion constraints.
Persist upload ID, part number, offset, size, checksum, ETag, attempt, and owner so completion uses the exact accepted part list.
Bound part workers and bytes, cancel safely, drain results, preserve completed evidence, and prevent goroutine or buffer leaks.

modules.title: Presigned Browser Uploads, POST Policies, and CORS
modules.description: A browser receives a broad week-long upload URL, changes signed headers, overwrites a shared key, and mistakes CORS success for storage authorization. Learners cumulatively produce least-privilege direct-upload session contract through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Create a one-object upload intent with tenant, generated key, expected size, type, checksum, expiry, status, and replay policy before signing.
Presign PUT with exact region, expiry, key, checksum, content type, encryption, and required header parity.
Use a POST policy when browser-enforced key, size, metadata, and field conditions are required and validate the completed object independently.

modules.title: HeadObject, GetObject, Conditions, Ranges, and Cleanup
modules.description: A download service calls GET to discover metadata, leaks response bodies, retries after sending headers, and returns full private objects for malformed ranges. Learners cumulatively produce bounded S3 download decision and cleanup flow through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Choose HEAD, GET, conditional GET, or a metadata database lookup without doubling requests or trusting stale authority.
Check response and metadata, own and close the body, propagate cancellation, handle partial reads, and distinguish transport from caller writes.
Map application validators and preconditions to S3 requests and preserve 304, 412, not-found, and version-specific meaning.

modules.title: Go HTTP Upload and Download Gateways
modules.description: A Go handler buffers multipart forms and S3 objects in memory, trusts proxy headers, writes status too early, and continues transferring after the client leaves. Learners cumulatively produce streaming Go file-gateway handler through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Apply trusted authority, authentication, authorization, method, route, headers, body, multipart, rate, and concurrency admission before storage work.
Use MaxBytesReader or bounded readers and streaming multipart iteration without ParseMultipartForm disk or memory surprises.
Delay response commitment until safe, set representation headers, stream bounded data, and record partial-write terminal states.

modules.title: Application Metadata, Transactions, Events, and Orphans
modules.description: A database row says ready before S3 accepts bytes, an object exists without an owner row after rollback, and events race ahead of scan and authorization state. Learners cumulatively produce file metadata and object reconciliation state machine through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Assign authoritative fields to application database, S3 object state, tags, events, or derived indexes and document reconciliation.
Persist idempotent intent, reservation, object identity, expected integrity, upload, quarantine, publish, failure, and expiry transitions.
Choose database-first, object-first, or direct-upload ordering and expose each crash window, compensation, and user-visible state.

modules.title: Copy, Replace, Rename, Delete, Batch, and Concurrency
modules.description: A rename is presented as atomic, replacement loses metadata, delete hides a live version, and concurrent writers silently overwrite each other. Learners cumulatively produce conditional object-mutation protocol through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Copy or replace with explicit source version, conditional evidence, metadata directive, encryption, checksum, tags, and destination validation.
Implement rename as version-aware copy, validation, metadata update, reference switch, and safe source deletion with recoverable intermediate states.
Use validators, version IDs, idempotency records, or application locks to detect stale concurrent mutation and expose 409 or 412 meaning.

modules.title: Versioning, Delete Markers, Object Lock, and Recovery
modules.description: A team enables versioning after an incident, assumes delete markers are backups, and applies compliance retention without testing irreversible governance and restore procedures. Learners cumulatively produce version and immutable-retention recovery runbook through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Distinguish unversioned, enabled, and suspended versioning and test null versions, overwrite behavior, and lifecycle impact.
List versions and delete markers, resolve current reads, restore by removing a marker or copying a version, and preserve audit identity.
Apply governance, compliance, legal hold, retention date, and authorized bypass by version with irreversible-risk review.

modules.title: Lifecycle, Storage Classes, Archive Restore, and Cost
modules.description: A policy transitions active content immediately, expires current versions but not hidden history, and promises instant archive access without retrieval or minimum-duration cost. Learners cumulatively produce lifecycle and storage-cost decision table through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Select storage class from access frequency, object size, retrieval latency, resilience, minimum duration, monitoring, and transition cost.
Model filters, current and noncurrent transitions, expiration, delete markers, multipart cleanup, precedence, propagation, and unsupported bucket features.
Request, monitor, authorize, and communicate temporary archive restoration with tier, expiry, duplicate request, and failure states.

modules.title: IAM, Bucket Policies, Ownership, Access Points, and Public Access
modules.description: A bucket is private by convention, one application role has s3:*, tenant prefixes are interpolated from user input, and ACLs conflict with ownership and public-block assumptions. Learners cumulatively produce least-privilege S3 authorization proof through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Evaluate identity and resource policies by principal, action, exact resource, condition, session, explicit deny, and organization boundary.
Enforce Block Public Access and bucket-owner-enforced Object Ownership while explaining what each does and does not authorize.
Derive tenant-safe keys and list prefixes server-side and bind allowed actions and conditions without trusting user-supplied policy fragments.

modules.title: TLS, SSE-S3, SSE-KMS, Bucket Keys, and Encryption Boundaries
modules.description: A service labels default encryption end-to-end, grants a broad KMS key policy, logs encryption context, and cannot rotate or revoke access without breaking unknown consumers. Learners cumulatively produce encryption and key-authorization contract through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Separate TLS in transit, S3 server-side encryption, KMS envelope encryption, and application client-side encryption by threat and feature tradeoff.
Choose SSE-S3, SSE-KMS, dual-layer KMS where required, or client encryption from audit, tenancy, performance, cost, and service integration needs.
Constrain IAM and KMS key policy, grants, service principal, encryption context, region, rotation, disable, deletion, and break-glass ownership.

modules.title: Presigned Downloads, CloudFront Signatures, Cookies, and Revocation
modules.description: A product places long-lived S3 presigned URLs in logs, uses one shared CloudFront key, signs with legacy assumptions, and promises immediate revocation after links escape. Learners cumulatively produce time-bounded signed-delivery decision record through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Presign the exact version, response metadata, method, region, credential scope, and shortest useful expiry while treating the URL as a bearer secret.
Choose signed URL or signed cookie by object count, path scope, player behavior, client constraints, and leakage surface.
Choose canned or custom CloudFront policy and constrain expiry, optional start time, resource wildcard, and IP range without accidental overbreadth.

modules.title: CloudFront Origins, OAC, SSE-KMS, and Private Delivery
modules.description: A distribution points at an S3 website endpoint, retains legacy OAI by habit, permits the CloudFront service principal globally, and forgets the KMS key policy. Learners cumulatively produce private CloudFront-to-S3 origin contract through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Distinguish regular S3 origins from website endpoints by HTTPS, OAC, redirects, error pages, methods, and public-access requirements.
Configure OAC with always-sign where appropriate and define viewer protocol, origin protocol, request signing, and forwarded Authorization behavior.
Scope the CloudFront service principal to the exact distribution SourceArn and required object actions without public or cross-distribution access.

modules.title: CloudFront Cache Keys, Origin Requests, TTLs, and Invalidation
modules.description: A cache key includes every viewer header, authorization is omitted, the origin receives different data than the cache varies on, and releases rely on global invalidation as routine deployment. Learners cumulatively produce cache-key, forwarding, and freshness contract through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Include only representation-changing path, query, header, cookie, encoding, and authorization dimensions in the cache key.
Use origin request policy for additional required viewer data without unnecessary cache fragmentation or trust escalation.
Combine origin Cache-Control, minimum, default, maximum, stale behavior, validators, and error caching with content-change and privacy requirements.

modules.title: Media Delivery, Range Requests, MP4, HLS, and Streaming Boundaries
modules.description: A video player downloads whole large files for seeking, an origin emits invalid range responses, MP4 metadata sits at the end, and private segment access differs from manifest access. Learners cumulatively produce accessible media-delivery and range plan through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Meet ascending, nonoverlapping, valid Range behavior, understand larger origin fetches, 200 fallbacks, chunked-origin limits, and cache chunking.
Prepare correct media type, length, validators, byte ranges, and fast-start MP4 metadata for progressive download and seeking.
Model manifests, variant playlists, segments, codecs, durations, naming, cache lifetimes, and atomic publication for adaptive delivery.

modules.title: Edge Security, WAF, Hotlinking, Headers, and Failure Containment
modules.description: A public distribution trusts Referer as authorization, caches private errors, lacks browser security headers, and forwards bot bursts to an expensive origin. Learners cumulatively produce layered edge-security and abuse-control plan through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Apply WAF, rate limits, bot controls, request size and method rules with false-positive, cost, accessibility, and bypass evidence.
Treat Referer hotlink controls as a weak cost signal and use signed access or a controlled origin header only within its actual threat boundary.
Set HSTS, MIME sniffing, frame, referrer, content security, cross-origin, and content-disposition headers appropriate to file types and embedding.

modules.title: Replication, Multi-Region Access, Backup, and Disaster Recovery
modules.description: A team equates regional S3 durability with application recovery, enables replication without version or KMS testing, and promises zero data loss and instant failover. Learners cumulatively produce RPO, RTO, replication, failover, and restore dossier through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Separate object durability, request availability, regional reachability, accidental deletion, corruption, account compromise, and application recovery.
Configure versioning, destination ownership, IAM, KMS, filters, delete-marker behavior, replication status, metrics, and failure remediation.
Evaluate Multi-Region Access Points, latency routing, active-active writes, consistency, replication lag, failover controls, and client endpoint support.

modules.title: Events, Observability, Capacity, Inventory, and Cost Control
modules.description: Operators have high-cardinality object-key metrics, duplicate unordered events, incomplete request traces, no inventory reconciliation, and no alert tied to a user or cost outcome. Learners cumulatively produce storage telemetry, inventory, capacity, and cost model through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Consume S3 notifications through supported destinations with duplicate, ordering, filtering, recursion, schema, delay, and lost-consumer recovery limits.
Correlate application operation, AWS request IDs, CloudTrail data events where justified, access logs, CloudFront logs, and stakeholder result without secrets.
Measure admitted and completed bytes, latency, failures, cache hit, range behavior, retries, backlog, replication, restore, and spend with bounded labels.

modules.title: Testing, Fault Injection, Release, and Production Defense
modules.description: A service passes mocked unit tests and a local emulator demo, then deploys new SDK and CDN settings without race, AWS contract, security, load, rollback, restore, or cost evidence. Learners cumulatively produce production file-delivery defense dossier through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Use table, property, and fuzz tests for key, range, policy, lifecycle, state-machine, checksum, retry, and cache invariants with changed cases.
Use fakes and emulators for deterministic feedback while listing unsupported AWS semantics and assigning controlled integration tests.
Gate formatting, vet, static analysis, tests, fuzz seeds, race, goroutine and body leaks, benchmarks, profiles, reproducible build, and dependency review.
